sentences.text: That's right.
So everybody was for himself.
And all of a sudden, I didn't have nobody.
I-- I had a horse.
But I was watching the horse better than myself.
You know, because if you don't feed them, they go.
And I didn't have nobody to connect with, you know, no Jews, and no-- well, one day came to me a guy.
We were running, you know from the-- from the Russians, from we didn't know from whom.
Anyway, from everybody.
There came to me a guy.
He says to me, you are Jewish, he says to me.
I say, how do you know?
He says, I know you.
You're from the-- from the-- the third-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [POLISH] in Polish.
But I forgot anyway.
Anyway, I say, how do you know.
He says, I know you're Jewish.
I said, who are you?
He says, I'm Tatar.
A Tatar, you know what a Tatar is?
A Tatar, it's like a-- like a Muslim, a Muslim.
And I don't know what it is.
Anyway, he felt that he-- if he's Tatar and I'm Jewish, we should be brothers.
He says, what are you doing?
You're Jewish.
What are you going-- I was running like I was excited.
I was running with the other guys.
I-- I didn't know where we were.
And then we find out we're going to Armenia, you know, the soldiers.
He says, let's go home.
What are you doing?
So I went with him.
We left everybody.
And I'll tell you a secret.
If you got a horse, and you're running with horses, you cannot go away from them.
The horse wouldn't let you go away from the-- from the group.
So he used to start-- he-- he-- he didn't want to go.
And he used to make a noise.
So we decided, me and the Tatar decided to get rid of the horses.
If you get rid of the horses, you have to walk.
In the daytime, we let them out.
We didn't know like-- no.
A lot of the guys used to walk.
Because they didn't have no horses.
And they were dying to have some transportation.
So you want him for a while, go ahead.
Ride with my horse.
And he did the same thing.
So-- but if you got rid of the horse, you will never see the horse with a guy together.
You know it.
So we did-- in this way we got rid from the horses and start walking.
And you know, this-- this was maybe a thousand miles.
Who knows?
Or kilometers, we started walking.
And they-- the Russian used the catch the people, and put them together, and then send them on the other side of the-- of the-- on the frontier, used to be Russian on the other side.
It was Communism over there.
And over there, they took us to a place.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Over there, maybe a million people-- not a million-- it was maybe thousands Polish soldiers, you know, like cattle.
And they didn't-- they didn't tell you nothing to do.
They didn't give you no place to sleep.
And-- and then they-- and they didn't give you nothing to eat.
All of sudden, I see people.
I saw people from Vilna, older people.
They was reservists, you know.
I say, what's the matter with you?
They don't need [INAUDIBLE].
They didn't need.
They lost their strength.
I saw what's going on.
And I was healthy like a-- like a bull.
So I figure if I don't get out of there, I'm not going to be able to get out of here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And-- and to make it short, I was the only one, hundreds of thousands, but not resisting, the people.
Nobody got out of there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah.
But I went out, and I escaped from over there.
That's another story.
I was scared of hunger.
So what they-- they didn't have themselves what to eat.
So how can-- you couldn't expect they should give you something.
So in the beginning, I started working over there.
And I work a couple days.
Then we had to march back to Poland, you know.
So they say, we're taking you home.
So we believe them.
We were working in the place.
At night we came in.
I see there's people there.
They got some sheds, you know, to sleep.
I say, what are we going to do here?
I used to speak Russian.
He says, what do you mean what are you going to do here?
You're going to work here.
The trains used to be-- the-- you call-- what do you call it?
The railroad?
Yeah.
The Polish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were smaller this way than the Russian.
And the Russians wanted to take over Poland.
They had to make wider the-- so I see, this will take me a year maybe.
Let me go out of here.
You couldn't get out.
They didn't give you nothing to eat.
He-- when he told me this, and I was on the mountain, and it was raining.
Something gave me like a needle, and I start running.
And I was from the first guys, when they came in there.
You know why?
Because I was going over there in the sides, and there were-- what do they call it?
They didn't have their own food for the pigs.
They have to-- to feed a pig, you have to cook the-- the-- the potatoes for him.
And then they used to drop it in places like this, you know.
And the pigs used to eat it.
So-- but the-- a pig has got a big nose, you know, a big-- you should know.
So he couldn't get in-- in-- in the corners, you know.
So he used to leave it in the corner.
And we were really hungry.
We used to put-- with the fingers, you know.
So but this, you couldn't get it, if you were farther away.
Because people were after us.
But we were the first ones, the smart ones.
So we had at least that's it, for the-- the leftover from the pigs.
Yeah.
And then we walked and walked.
And I felt that we were going the right way, you know, like west, you know.
It means to Poland, back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I think I got rid of them-- all the Poles were there.
And we went west.
And I felt we are going in the right way.
Because on the-- on the highways, all we see-- I see old men.
And dressed, didn't have no-- no-- no-- no-- no clothing.
You know clothing, like but civilian clothing.
Old man, it means maybe he was 40 years old.
You know, it looked then-- and it was raining.
And they were tired.
Because they didn't have enough food.
The same thing like we didn't have it.
I feel-- like over there it's west.
And I had to escape.
Because how-- how long can you eat over there, from-- from the pig?
[LAUGHS] And then it was night, and we came to a highway.
And then I see-- they're going after-- I figure they're going after me, you know?
Who is escaping?
Me, I was an escapee.
But no, they were going into Poland.
I see the people are black, dirty like me.
Everybody the same thing, and all the people, I figure this is-- they go to Poland, you know.
I stand up, and I mix through the people.
And they didn't even look at me.
I tear off my-- my things, you know.
What did you tear off?
My-- my not medals, but it was a-- like-- [NON-ENGLISH], what do you say-- what do you say, in the-- in the sky, you know the one that flies and catches?
The Polish emblem.
We have-- you know, like an--
Insignia, yeah.
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is what is it?
You're not allowed to hunt him now?
Eagles, yeah.
This was an eagle, you know.
And we took off all the buttons.
And I was laying there.
And I figure, that's it.
And I stand up, with the-- with the guys.
They didn't even look at me.
They didn't have the strength.
They're soldiers.
They're Russian soldiers.
And we came to a place, and we sit down.
And they start eating.
And a guy asked me, did you eat?
I say, something.
He took off a piece of bread.
He gave me a piece of-- of pork.
And I ate.
All of a sudden, I became alive.
[LAUGHS] And we start going, you know, and it was pouring.
It was raining.
It was anything that you wanted.
And we came to a place.
All of sudden, we see lights.
This was the frontier, the Polish-Russian frontier.
But still they had it.
Later on they took it away, anyway.
The Russians took away this.
And they-- they get up.
And we were walking, you know.
And nobody looked at us.
Who's going to look at a bunch of [INAUDIBLE]?
And we were walking.
And it was-- it was already getting light.
We-- we came in, in the town.
And I was walking on the sidewalk.
And it was before I had done, you know, the-- I stepped off of the sidewalk in-- in the hole, like of two family houses over there, small houses, you know.
And I close the door.
And I went upstairs, you know.
I'm on the second floor.
Nobody's up yet.
And I went near the-- near the attic.
And I sitting there, waiting.
I didn't know what-- for what.
But I was sitting there.
All of a sudden I hear, [NON-ENGLISH] Yiddish, from the houses.
This was like-- two-- or four family houses, you know.
I figure, they're waking up.
They open up.
I say, I'm Jewish.
You got something to eat?
This was [INAUDIBLE].
You know, I said, be careful because they're catching the soldiers, you know, and they take him away.
So we're sitting there.
All of a sudden, they gave me [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH] is?
Made from-- from-- from potatoes, you know, like-- like a-- a flat one.
You bake it from potatoes, you know.
With-- not-- not even-- you don't know what [NON-ENGLISH] is?
They got [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH] is?
[NON-ENGLISH] is made from-- he doesn't know either?
Is it like kugel?
Yeah.
But [NON-ENGLISH] is-- from of what is made kugel?
It can made from potatoes or from [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, so that's it.
So I see it was a holiday.
[LAUGHS] So I ask--
Sure, it was a holiday.
All of a sudden--
I think maybe Rosh Hashanah.
I don't know.
But when do they eat kugel?
I don't know.
And I was sitting there all day.
I think I have to go.
I-- I would give everything away.
But I wouldn't give away my boots.
My boots were new boots.
I had it from-- from-- it protects you, you know.
And it was comfortable.
I-- I gave everything away, if somebody wanted to.
I mean if they wanted to, what I had, what's worthwhile, anyway.
I was waiting till it got dark.
I ask the people, where-- where am I?
I didn't know where I am.
Vilna I knew it was up-- up, up in the north.
But they had a city-- I was learning in this [NON-ENGLISH] I told you, over there it used to from all Poland used to come to study in this school.
So I had a couple friends from Rowne.
And so I have to go someplace.
If somebody asks me, where are you going to go.
I say Rowne.
What was the name of this town?
The town where I was there?
I don't remember what it was.
It was in the south of Poland.
Rowne is a big city.
Anyway, I said, we have to go to Rowne.
I start walking.
So I started walking, and it was without end.
I had to go-- I had to go in-- at nighttime, because I was afraid of the Russians.
They'll catch me, they'll send me to the-- If I-- if I'll take off my shoes-- my boots and everything, they-- they'll think I'm-- I'm a thief who works at night.
And nobody will give you to eat nothing.
You ask for something to eat.
This way, I had my-- my clothing.
I used to knock on the door.
I used to say I'm a soldier.
I'm lost.
I have to go home.
They used to give you something to eat.
So I used to walk nights.
I didn't walk in the daytime, because I was afraid of the Russian.
At night, I used to walk.
I was walking like this, I don't know, maybe without end.
And I-- and then I came near Rowne.
Rowne, that's a Jewish city, where people were speaking Jewish.
There was a guy who used to drive a truck or something-- I don't know-- not a truck-- and I asked him where is-- do you know where Rowne is?
He says, not far, 50 kilometers.
He says to me, you want to go with-- I'm going to Rowne tomorrow, he says.
He said-- then when I came over there.
He says, this will cost you 50 zlotys.
I didn't have not 50 cents.
I didn't see how it looks, anyway.
I said, OK, I'll pay you in Rowne.
[LAUGHS]
He says, OK.
But he wouldn't let you in, to sit-- on the thing.
You had to walk behind.
What do you think?
For-- 50 zlotys, he should let you ride.
So we walked like this.
And we came in Rowne.
And it was-- then on the way, he got another couple guys like me.
You see right away who it is.
So they had some money ready.
Were they Jewish too?
Yeah.
Sure, who else?
We came to Rowne.
So he took us to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] is?
Where they-- they got a couple temples in one place, you know.
What they call it?
Anyway, he took us over there.
And he says, to me he says, you have got your friends.
You go to them, and take their money or something.
So I was staying there.
There came out a boy, a young boy.
And do you know-- what was his name, the guy who was learning with me in the [NON-ENGLISH]?
Grossman or-- anyway, nobody knew.
And this boy, he says, I know it where it is.
He took me over there.
I see a beautiful house, and everything.
I knock on the door.
Where is my friend, you know?
He's not here.
Where is he?
He's in the army.
But they send him in another place, you know, down.
And me they send up.
And the-- the people there-- I would-- like-- look like, you know, I didn't wash myself for who knows how long?
Anyway, they gave me a place to sleep over.
And-- the next day, they wanted to get rid of me.
Because the guy, he wasn't there.
And who wants to have a guy like-- so then they knew a friend, another friend of mine from Vilna.
So they sent over the maid.
She went over there.
And she brought a guy, the other guy.
He recognized me right away.
So he took me over there.
He didn't-- he-- I don't know what he did.
He didn't have nothing.
Anyway, he didn't invite me.
I say, I want to go to Vilna.
And they had the-- the street-- they burn lights.
You know, they were working, like in a regular place.
But he didn't have no money to give me.
I-- I didn't have a penny.
So we went out.
All of a sudden, I see.
He took me to a place like the people were coming over there and doing business, you know.
And I see he shows to the people that over there, this is the guy.
I didn't know it in the beginning, then I find out he was collecting money to buy a ticket for me.
So he bought me a ticket.
And he give me a couple-- I didn't have a penny.
I didn't know what it is even.
And he gave me a couple zlotys, they used to call it.
And I bought myself a loaf of bread, and a piece of salami.
And here is the train.
[LAUGHS] You got on the train?
I got on the train.
I was supposed to go to Vilna.
And we-- we're going with the train maybe 50 miles or something like this.
After, everybody out.
Because they needed train for-- for wounded soldiers, but not me.
I was standing over near to-- I was standing over there a week.
I didn't go out.
And I came-- but I was smarter.
I knew if I got to Vilna, they'll expect me on-- on the station.
Because I figured they're catching all soldiers.
So I went down a station before, like something, a couple of miles from-- from the city.
I walked again.
And I came in.
And I thought, I see nobody moves.
So I'm thinking, maybe something happened.
It was on Saturday.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I came in.
And I came to my-- my place, even though-- so I was afraid.
I figured, who knows?
Maybe everybody is dead.
That's the way it felt, you know.
I was waiting.
And I see a woman, and I knew she knows my parents.
And I told her, go in, please.
And tell them you saw my-- my-- your son over there.
Don't tell them where.
And he came in and told them.
And they came out, and then-- and they fall.
And they knew I'm dead already.
And all of a sudden, we come back.
And-- and then it started again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Russians give back, the-- the government-- the Lithuanian-- the whole country, they give it back to the-- to the-- to the Lithuanian.
And this one belonged [? excuse me ?] before.
And Poland and Russia, they were fighting always.
And all of a sudden, I see on bicycles came in the-- the Lithuanian government on bicycles, 3-4 bicycles.
And behind them were Poles already.
All of sudden, the make up a [INAUDIBLE] on the Jews.
This was the way it was going.
And I came, just I couldn't see it.
How can the-- this garbage fight against the Jewish [INAUDIBLE]?
I took a-- how do they say?
You-- you knock the-- hole in the wood, you know, you split-- the split the wood.
I catch the [NON-ENGLISH], and they see me and my brother, my family, like cutting.
I guess that with-- with one person they were maybe six or seven.
And I went out.
I was crazy against them, you know, and start fighting with them.
They're used to-- they escaped everybody.
And my father with my brother, they catch me.
Took away from me, the-- the thing that--
Yeah, an ax.
And then this is where it started.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, this was Shabbos.
And on Shabbos you eat, you start eating, you know.
You start eating.
So I was sick of eating.
I was afraid that we wouldn't have enough food tomorrow.
So I was-- start talking to my mother.
I say, don't you have-- are you not afraid that you wouldn't have nothing tomorrow?
She says I'm-- I'm crazy.
You know, I was scared.
I say, you have to make hard the bread, you know, and bake it.
She shouldn't get the-- what they call-- Anyway, she said I'm crazy.
She start baking, making it for me, you know.
I was standing so much.
I should eat.
I start-- I couldn't stop eating.
That's a sickness.
And that's the way it started, you know.
And then the German came.
I was a technical-- anything technical was my-- my specialty.
So I got a specialist.
You needed a plumber?
I am a plumber.
You need-- anything you needed, I-- I could do it, electrician?
I was that.
So I got a job over there.
And in the beginning we worked in-- not far from the house.
The Germans came?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember.
Anyway, they came.
Well, it started the-- the war, you know, between the-- the-- the Russian and the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we didn't suffer, you know.
We had everything good.
We had a-- the store, the grocery store.
We had whiskey in it, you know.
And when the-- the Russians came in, [BACKROUND NOISE] or the German, I forgot, anyway.
So we brought it in from-- from the store, we brought it in the house.
And then we hide it behind the curtain.
And when the-- the Germans came, they used to take away all the men from the houses, and take them away.
They didn't say where or what.
They say, they have to-- he's getting to work.
They went to Ponar.
Ponar was the place where they used to kill the people, the Jewish people.
My father had-- we had this-- the whiskey, in the house, you know, under a curtain.
And then the Germans came in.
They came in maybe four or five, you know, in the house.
They used to take away the men.
When he saw the-- the-- the place with the whiskey over there.
He says, I want to take it, he says.
But I cannot take it.
Because I have the men.
And my father said, come later.
So he-- he didn't take the men with him.
We had a couple-- a couple of plating-- [NON-ENGLISH], we call it, who escaped from Poland to us, you know, to Vilna.
So they used to live with us too.
Because he was a friend of my brother.
They didn't take no men from our apartment.
And when they took us to the ghettos, you know, we had to take any bundles you could-- you could carry and take it with you.
How much can you carry from your house with people?
But we took it.
When we went out, we were sitting on a-- on the sidewalk, and waiting to somebody to show us how to go to-- to the ghetto.
All of a sudden, we had so many tenants, no men.
We were the only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] house who had a man, you know, we had three or four men.
And the doctor was upstairs, and he had two-- two men.
This was from the whole house.
This was an apartment house, a new apartment house.
